title: Local distillation from Reed Muller codes unfolding
abstract: We generalize the unfolding of a Reed Muller distillation factory of Ruiz et. al. by exhibiting the algebraic structure that the unfolding is based on. We describe a 2D local layout for the Z stabilizers of a distance 4 Reed Muller distillation factory and a 3D local layout for the Z stabilizer of a distance 4 and a distance 7 Reed Muller distillation factory. Given input T states with infidelities $p=10^{-3}$, the 2D local distillation factory with distance 4 outputs a CCZ state with infidelity $p=8.256 \times 10^{-9}$ and the 3D local distillation factory with distance 7 outputs a T state with infidelity $p=1.1811 \times 10^{-17}$.
summary: This paper provides a mathematical framework for arranging quantum error-correcting code components into local 2D and 3D architectures. By 'unfolding' high-dimensional Reed-Muller codes, it enables more efficient magic state distillation with significantly reduced logical error rates.
main_problem: The paper aims to generalize the 'unfolding' of Reed-Muller distillation factories to achieve local 2D and 3D layouts for Z stabilizers, providing an algebraic basis for these structures.
main_result: The author derives 2D and 3D local layouts for Z stabilizers of distance 4 and distance 7 Reed-Muller codes and demonstrates significant improvements in magic state distillation fidelity.
method: The work uses an algebraic framework involving polynomials over finite fields (F2) and Gray code ordering to transform high-dimensional hypercube descriptions into lower-dimensional local layouts.
model_or_system: The study focuses on Quantum Reed-Muller (QRM) codes, specifically analyzing the stabilizer groups and logical operations of codes like QRM(4,1), QRM(6,1), and QRM(7,2) within a noise-biased model (e.g., cat qubits).
key_observables: Logical error probability, stabilizer group dimension, minimum distance (dZ), and output infidelity of distilled T and CCZ states.
important_parameters: Input infidelity (p=10^-3), code distance, number of logical qubits, and the prefactor of the logical error probability.
main_assumptions: The efficiency of the local layouts assumes a noise-biased model where the X stabilizer group does not necessarily require locality.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the geometric unfolding of stabilizers, showing 2D planar layouts, 3D 'Rubik's Cube' layouts, and the progression of building stabilizer bases using subcubes and Gray code ordering.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the unfolding problem, provides an algebraic derivation of the stabilizer structure, details the construction of 2D and 3D layouts, analyzes the logical action of Clifford hierarchy operators, and concludes with error performance analysis.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 3: The cartesian product of a basis for edges in the 2-cube with coordinate indices 1 and 2 by a basis for edges in the 2-cube with coordinate indices 3 and 4 yields a basis for the space generated by squares of type {i, j} with i ∈{1, 2} and j ∈{3, 4} in the 4-cube. As depicted in green on Table 2, the basis is made of 4 squares of type {1, 4}, 2 squares of type {1, 3}, 2 squares of type {2, 4} and 1 square of type {2, 3}. To generate the full Z stabilizer group of QRM4(1, 1), Table 2 shows that a square of type {1, 2} and a square of type {3, 4} must be appended to this basis. The square of type {3, 4} is the upper-left square of Figure 2. It corresponds to the polynomial (X1 +...

Figure 4: The 4 vertices of a 2-cube (i.e. a square) are grouped on a single axis (North- West/South-East) with a Gray code ordering. Each vertex is labeled x1x2. Edges are labeled with the (degree 1) polynomial P such that P(v) = 1 if and only if v is one of the two vertices incident to this edge. Lemma 3.1 shows that the 3 edges of the Figure form a basis of (F2[X1, X2])≤1.

Figure 5: The 4 nodes on each subfigure represent the 4 coordinate indices of the 4-cube. A type of square is characterized by 2 coordinate indices and corresponds therefore to an edge on this Figure. There are 4 squares of each type. It is sufficient to add a lower power of 2 (i.e. 2 or 1) number of squares of each type to obtain a basis of RM(4, 1). The edge label is the number of squares of a given type in the basis of RM(4, 1) depicted in Figure 3. Figure (a) corresponds to the top right entry of Table 1. Figure (b) corresponds to the top right entry of Table 1 and the first diagonal beneath it. Figure (c) corresponds all entries of Table 1.

Figure 6: Planar layout of a set of generators of the Z stabilizer group of QRM6(1, 1). Each vertex is a physical qubit. Each square is a Z stabilizer of weight 4. Each half-round shape is a Z stabilizer of weight 2. Half-round shapes are used to make every weight 4 stabilizer into a geometrical square. They are not described in the main text.
